coredata.dc:title: One-Step Electrosynthesis and Photoelectric Conversion of Selenium Nanowires Wrapped with Graphene Quantum Dots
coredata.prism:copyright: Copyright © 2015 Elsevier Ltd. All rights reserved.
coredata.dc:creator: Zhang, Xiaomei
Ke, Rui
Zhang, Shengyi
Niu, Helin
Song, Jiming
Mao, Changjie
Jin, Baokang
Tian, Yupeng
coredata.dc:description: Using graphene quantum dots (GQDs) and H2SeO3 as precursors, the reduced graphene quantum dots/Se nanowires (rGQDs/SeNWs) composite was electro-synthesized in the presence of cetyltrimethylammonium bromide (CTAB). The morphology and crystallization of the products as-synthesized was studied by a series of characterization techniques. The conductivity and photoresponse of rGQDs, Se NWs and rGQDs/SeNWs composite were determined respectively. Benefiting from the synergistic action of electron-deficient rGQDs and electron-rich Se NWs, significantly, the rGQDs/SeNWs composite showed excellent photoelectric conversion.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Selenium nanowires
Graphene quantum dots
Composite
Photoelectric conversion

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: Diagram for electrosynthesis of rGQDs/SeNWs composite.

(A) The cycle voltammograms obtained in different precursor solutions (at scan rate 1mV/s); (B) XRD patterns of the electroreduction products obtained: (a) in GQDs solution; (b) in H2SeO3 solution; (c) in GQDs/H2SeO3 mixture solution; (C) TEM image of the product obtained by electrooxidation of graphite; (D) SEM image of the electroreduction product obtained in GQDs solution; (E) SEM image of the electroreduction product obtained in H2SeO3 solution containing CTAB (insert: enlarge TEM image of single wire); (F) SEM image of the rGQDs/SeNWs composite (upper right insert: enlarged TEM image of single wire; bottom left insert: enlarged SEM image of single wire).

(A) UV–vis absorption spectra of the products, insert: photographs of products on ITO; (B) Nyquist plots of EIS for products (at the frequency range of 0.1Hz–100KHz and the amplitude of 5mV). Letters in figures: a: rGQDs; b: Se NWs; c: rGQDs/SeNWs; d: bare ITO.
(A) Photocurrent profiles under illumination: (a) rGQDs, (b) Se NWs, (c) rGQDs/SeNWs; (B) Schematic illustration for photoelectric conversion of the rGQDs/SeNWs composite.

SEM images of the rGQDs/SeNWs composite obtained: (A) electrodeposition for 1200s; (B) electrodeposition for 2100s; (C) electrodeposition at −1.0V; (D) electrodeposition at −1.5V; (E) no CTAB in electrolyte solution; (F) in electrolyte solution containing 0.01M H2SeO3; (G) in electrolyte solution containing 0.10M CTAB; (H) in pH4 electrolyte solution; (I) in pH8 electrolyte solution.

Photoelectric conversion curves of the products obtained by: (A) different electroreduction time (as inset); (B) different electroreduction potential (as inset); (C) different H2SeO3 concentration (as inset); (D) using 0.1M KI as electrolyte in photoelectric conversion test.
(A) Patterns of photocurrents versus time; (B) Patterns of photocurrents versus voltage.

head.ce:title: One-Step Electrosynthesis and Photoelectric Conversion of Selenium Nanowires Wrapped with Graphene Quantum Dots

ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Department of Chemistry, Anhui University, Hefei 230601, China

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Using graphene quantum dots (GQDs) and H2SeO3 as precursors, the reduced graphene quantum dots/Se nanowires (rGQDs/SeNWs) composite was electro-synthesized in the presence of cetyltrimethylammonium bromide (CTAB). The morphology and crystallization of the products as-synthesized was studied by a series of characterization techniques. The conductivity and photoresponse of rGQDs, Se NWs and rGQDs/SeNWs composite were determined respectively. Benefiting from the synergistic action of electron-deficient rGQDs and electron-rich Se NWs, significantly, the rGQDs/SeNWs composite showed excellent photoelectric conversion.

ce:section.ce:para: Since discovered in 2004, graphene as a two-dimensional (2D) honeycomb network of carbon atoms, has received much attention due to its extraordinary properties, such as large specific surface area, high electronic conductivity, and good chemical stability [1]. In recent years, ultrafine graphene quantum dots (GQDs) are known as a new kind of quantum dots and have ignited tremendous research interest [2–8]. Generally, the GQDs are single carbon layer nano-materials whose sheets are smaller than graphene and have oxygen-containing functional groups at the edges [9–10]. Comparing with semiconductor quantum dots (SQDs), the GQDs show low toxicity, high stability, good solubility, high electrical and thermal conductivity [11–15]. The merits mentioned above make GQDs promising materials in a variety of fields, such as energy conversion, bioanalysis and sensors. Generally, the synthesis methods of GQDs are classified into two groups: bottom-up and top-down approaches [16]. The bottom-up approach makes use of complicated chemical synthesis of small molecules. For example, based on oxidative condensation reactions, large colloidal GQDs with a uniform and tunable size were synthesized through solution chemistry [17]. The blue luminescent GQDs that could be used as a sensor for the analysis of explosive substances were prepared from citric acid by a pyrolysis procedure [8]. The top-down approach covered a decomposition process of suitable carbon precursors. By the electrolysis of the graphite rod and then the reduction with hydrazine hydrate, for example, the water-soluble and high fluorescent GQDs were prepared [18]. Using reduced graphene oxide as precursor, the reduced GQDs (namely, rGQDs) were hydrothermally synthesized and used as a fluorescent sensing platform for highly efficient detection of copper(II) ions [7]. Recently, the various modifications for GQDs or rGQDs were developed, in order to improve their properties. Shen et al. developed a simple hydrothermal synthesis for preparing GQDs surface-passivated by polyethylene glycol (PEG), and the GQDs-PEG composite as-prepared showed excellent luminescence properties and high photocurrent generation capability [19]. Zhuo et al. prepared a TiO2/GQDs composite for the photo-degradation of methylene blue [20]. Yang et al. made the porous PtPd nanochains conjugated with GQDs, for improving the sensitivity of the electrochemiluminescence immunoassay [21].

As well-known, selenium (Se) as an important elemental semiconductor with a smaller band gap (1.7eV), has been extensively used in solar cells, semiconductor rectifiers, medical diagnostics and xerography, due to its physical properties such as photoconductivity, piezoelectricity, thermoelectricity, and nonlinear optical responses [22]. Since one-dimensional (1D) nanostructures have unique properties and potential applications in electronic and photonic nanodevices, a lot of efforts have been devoted to the synthesis of Se materials with 1D nanostructures such as Se nanowires (NWs) and nanotubes [22–27]. Generally, the 1D Se materials and their composites were prepared by chemical solution phase approaches. For example, in order to fabricate the cathode material of rechargeable lithium batteries, the composite based-1D Se was synthesized in mixture solution containing Se nanofibers and graphene oxide by a hydrothermal process [28]. However, our group had prepared Se nanotubes by a simple, fast and eco-friendly electrochemical approach [29]. In this work, a strategy for one-step electrosynthesis of the composite based-Se NWs was proposed. The rGQDs/SeNWs composite as-synthesized showed the excellent photoelectric conversion performance that benefited from both the photosensitivity of Se NWs and the electroconductivity of rGQDs.

Selenium dioxide (≥99.0%) was purchased from Tianjin Fine Chemicals Co. Sodium sulphite anhydrous and Sodium sulfide nonahydrate were respectively obtained from Shanghai Zhen Xin Reagent Factory and Shanghai Tongya Chemical Technology Development Co. Ltd. Cetyltrimethylammonium bromide (CTAB) was purchased from Sinopharm Chemical Reagent Co. Ltd. All reagents were of analytical grade and used without further purification. Doubly distilled water was used throughout experiments.

Scanning electron microscope (SEM, S4800, Hitachi); Transmission electron microscopy (TEM, JEM-2100, JEOL, Japan); X-ray diffractometer (XRD, Rigaku D/max-RA, graphite monochromatized Cukα1 radiation, λ- 0.15406nm); Ultraviolet visible spectrophotometer (UV–vis, UV-1750, SHIMADZU, Japan); Electrochemical work station (IM6, ZAHNER, Germany; CHI660D, Shanghai Chenhua Instruments Ltd, China).

Consulting the methods reported [18,30], the preparation of GQDs was performed in an electrochemical cell consisting of a graphite rod (GR, 0.8cm in diameter) work electrode (WE), a platinum wire counter electrode (CE), a Ag/AgCl reference electrode (RE), and a 20mL 0.1M NaOH electrolyte solution. Typically, the potential applied on the GR electrode was set at 3.0V, and the electrooxidation time was set as 4h. During the electrolytic process, gradually, the color of electrolyte solution changed from colorless to dark-brown, which indicated that the GQDs were formed in solution.

In preparation, similar three-electrode system as above was used except that the GR electrode was replaced by a piece of the indium tin oxide glass (ITO, 4cm2) cleaned with acetone and ethanol respectively. Typically, the electrolyte solution (pH6) was prepared by adding 3mL GQDs solution and 1.1g CTAB into 9mL 0.03M H2SeO3 solution, and the electroreduction was carried out at −1.3V for 1800s. During the electroreduction process, gradually, the color of ITO electrode surface changed from colorless to brown, which indicated that the rGQDs/SeNWs composite was formed. For removing residual electrolyte, the composite formed on the ITO surface was washed by ethanol and water, and dried at room temperature. For comparison, in same electrochemical conditions as above, the pure rGQDs and Se NWs were electrodeposited on the surface of ITO electrode in GQDs solution and H2SeO3 solution containing CTAB, respectively.

The products as-prepared were characterized by SEM, TEM, XRD and UV–vis, respectively. In photoelectric conversion measurements, typically, three-electrode system was set up: a platinum wire (CE), an Ag/AgCl (RE) and the ITO electrode covered with rGQDs/SeNWs composite (WE). The mixture solution of 0.1M Na2S and 0.1M Na2SO3 was used as electrolyte. The photocurrent produced was recorded while the work electrode was being irradiated (150mW/cm2) by a xenon arc lamp. Nyquist plots of electrochemical impedance spectroscopy (EIS) were measured in 10mM PBS (pH=7.4) solution containing 2mM Fe(CN)6 4−/3−and 0.1M KCl, using same three-electrode system as above excepting that the Ag/AgCl electrode was replaced by saturated calomel electrode (SCE).

For the sake of finding suitable electroreduction potential, the cyclic voltammetry (CV) curves of the precursors for rGQDs/SeNWs composite were studied in advance and the results are shown in Fig. 1 A. According to the results reported [31–32], the CV curve of the GQDs obtained by oxidation is similar to that of graphene oxide, since there are a lot of active oxygen-containing groups on the edge of GQDs. Here, on the CV curve of our GQDs solution, there are a reduction peak at −1.20V and an oxidation peak at −0.72V. For H2SeO3 solution, there is a wide reduction peak at -0.75V on the CV curve, which resulted from two reduction reactions [33–34]:
H2SeO3 +6H+ +6e−
→ H2Se+3H2O
H2SeO3 +2H2Se+6e−
→ 3Se+3H2O

For mixture solution containing GQDs and H2SeO3, there are two reduction peaks (at −0.78V and −1.25V, respectively) and one oxidation peak (at −0.65V) on the CV curve, which may originate from the reduction of H2SeO3, the reduction and oxidation of GQDs, respectively. If the electrolyte solution containing GQDs and H2SeO3 was de-aired with N2 beforehand, the reduction peak (at −0.78V) declined largely, and another reduction peak (at −1.25V) was enhanced, as shown in Fig. 1A. The phenomenon indicated that there is dissolved oxygen in electrolyte solution. In terms of above cycle voltammograms of the electrolyte solution, the electroreduction potential for preparation of rGQDs/SeNWs composite was set at −1.30V where the rGQDs and elemental Se could be synchronously electrodeposited on the surface of the ITO plate.

The XRD patterns of the products obtained by electroreduction are shown in Fig. 1B. Here, it is worth pointing out that the ITO itself has diffraction peaks (marked as “△” in Fig. 1B) as reported [31]. On curve (a), there is a weak wide diffraction peak (at about 26°) that is a feature diffraction peak of reduced graphene [32], which suggests the formation of rGQDs. For Se NWs product, the diffraction peaks on curve (b) can be readily indexed to the trigonal phase of Se [35–37]. From the diffraction peaks on curve (c), it is speculated that the rGQDs/SeNWs composite was produced.

The TEM image reveals that the size of the GQDs produced by the electrooxidation of graphite is about 5nm, as shown in Fig. 1C. The GQDs formation mechanism is proposed as follows [18]. In electrooxidation, the O and OH radicals produced by anodic oxidation of water served as electrochemical “scissors” to cut carbon nanocrystals. Therefore, the GQDs were formed on the surface of graphite rod and then gradually entered into aqueous solution, since there are a lot of OH, CO and epoxide groups on the surface of the GQDs. Fig. 1D indicates that the GQDs dispersed in solution could be electrodeposited on the surface of ITO electrode and formed a stacking film of particles (rGQDs) whose size is about 10–100nm. Clearly, the size of rGQDs is much larger than that of GQDs, which means that the electroreduction of GQDs followed by growth of particles. The phenomenon is explained as that strong van der Waals interactions among reduced graphene quantum dots make them tend to aggregate and form larger uneven particles, similar to graphene sheets [1].

The SEM image (Fig. 1E) indicates that the Se NWs obtained in H2SeO3 solution containing CTAB have dozens of micron in length and 80nm in diameter. As shown insert in Fig. 1E, the crystal stripe of single nanowire means single crystalline. Also, as shown in Fig. 1F, the product with 1D structure was obtained in mixture solution of GQDs and H2SeO3-CTAB. However, as shown upper right and bottom left inserts in Fig. 1F, the Se nanowire in the product is covered by GQDs shell with 10nm in thickness, which indicates the formation of the rGQDs/SeNWs composite.

According to the composition and property of electrolyte solution, the possible formation mechanism of the rGQDs/SeNWs composite is proposed, as depicted in Scheme 1 . In advance, the GQDs solution was prepared by the electrooxidation of GR electrode as shown in Scheme 1(a). On this basis, the electrolyte solution was organized by adding CTAB and H2SeO3 into GQDs solution. As well known, cationic surfactant CTAB molecules are able to organize themselves into micelles by the alkyl chain interaction of the molecules. Since CTAB molecules are completely ionized in aqueous systems, the micelles bring along many positive charges in their polar shell [38]. In addition, the shape of the micelles can undergo sphere-to-rod transition along with the concentration increase (from 1mM up to 250mM), and the aromatic counterions are very effective in promoting micellar growth [39]. In our electrolyte solution for electroreduction, rod-like micelles could be formed since the CTAB concentration is high (250mM). Meanwhile, H2SeO3 (dissociation constants: Ka1 =3.5·10−5, Ka2 =5.0·10−8)existed as anionic HSeO3 − and the GQDs were negatively charged in electrolyte solution (pH6) [18]. As a result, HSeO3 − and GQDs were absorbed on the surface of cationic CTAB micelles by electrostatic force, as shown in Scheme 1(b). Based on the surfactivity and positive-charge of CTAB micelles, the GQDs and HSeO3 − loaded on CTAB micelles were taken to the surface of cathodic ITO, and then electro-reduced to rGQDs and elemental Se, respectively. In no time, amorphous Se (a-Se) or trigonal Se (t-Se) seeds were formed by the nucleation of Se adatoms, as shown in Scheme 1(c). Along with the electrolysis going on, t-Se seeds spontaneously grew along the c-axis due to the inherently anisotropic structure of t-Se, accompanied by production of new Se atoms and transformation of a-Se to t-Se. In addition, the deposition current density was not uniform owing to the formation of seeds and would be concentrated at the tip of the deposits, which favored directional growth of seeds [29]. Meanwhile, the produced rGQDs hold together by π–π electron interaction and van der Waals interaction. Therefore, Se nanowire wrapped by rGQDs shell was formed, as shown in Scheme 1(d).

It is worth pointing out that CTAB not only made the electrode reaction going on easily, but also promoted the formation of Se NWs [29]. First, CTAB is a cationic surfactant that acted as the electrolyte and increased the conductivity of electrolyte solution. Second, CTAB micelles adsorbed on the surface of ITO electrode formed electropositive passages that made the electrode reaction easy. Thirdly, CTAB micelles acted as the templates on which the Se NWs were developed. The function of CTAB was validated by the fact that the particulate or stub-like (not nanowires) product was produced if little or no CTAB was added in electrolyte solution, which will be discussed in follow section.

Fig. 2 A shows the UV–vis absorption spectra of the products. Consulting the results reported [40–42], the absorption band at 330nm on curve (a) is assigned to typical π→π* electronic transition absorption of rGQDs. For Se NWs product, there is a wide absorption band at 300–600nm on curve (b), which resulted from direct transitions between the energy levels, indirect interband transitions and interchain interactions caused by perpendicular to the c axis [43–44]. As shown on curve (c), comparing with rGQDs and Se NWs products, the rGQDs/SeNWs composite has a stronger and red-shifted absorption, which resulted from the fact that the interaction of Se NWs with rGQDs decreased the energy bandwidth of the composite. Here, the band gaps derived from the absorption spectra of rGQDs, Se NWs and rGQDs/SeNWs are 2.69, 1.94 and 1.86eV respectively. Insert in Fig. 2A shows the color of products deposited on ITO surface.

As well known, the EIS test is an effective method to monitor the charge transfer resistance change of the electrode surface. Generally, internal resistance (Rs) of the electrolyte and electrode can be derived from the point intersecting with the real axis in high frequency region in Nyquist plot [45]. The electrode surface electron transfer resistance (Ret) caused by the Faradaic reactions and the double-layer capacitance can be obtained from the semicircle-like part in high frequency region [46]. From the Nyquist plots of the EIS analysis for our electrodes, as shown in Fig. 2B, it can be calculated that the values of Rs/Ret are 71/50, 75/450, 77/60 and 66/145 (Ω), for bare ITO electrode, rGQDs electrode, Se NWs electrode and rGQDs/SeNWs electrode, respectively. Clearly, the internal resistances for different electrodes covered with products have not changed much. However, the rGQDs electrode has largest electron transfer resistance among electrodes, which may originate from the discontinuity of reduced graphene quantum dots. Since reduced graphene quantum dots were cross-linked at the surface of Se NWs and formed a continuous electroconductive shell, the rGQDs/SeNWs electrode has lower electron transfer resistance and internal resistance than rGQDs electrode.

The measurement results for the photoelectric conversion of the products are shown in Fig. 3 A. As shown on curve (a), the photocurrent produced by the rGQDs is very small, since the conductivity of reduced graphene quantum dots themselves made the photoelectrons easy to combine with holes [47]. In addition, the relatively low level of absorptivity and the short lifetimes of excitons for rGQDs are unfavorable to the photocurrent [48]. Also, the Se NWs produced a very small current under irradiation, which is explained as follows [49]. In principle, the origin of the photocurrent is due to generation of electron-hole pairs in Se NWs by irradiated photons with energy larger than the band gap of Se. At the outset, the photocurrent rapidly increased under illumination since all visible light has enough energy to produce electron-hole pairs on narrow band-gap Se NWs. However, the photo-generated electron-hole pairs immediately upset the original carrier balance and fill in the trap states existed in Se NWs, which leads to the decay of the photocurrent, as shown on curve (b). This phenomenon is supported by the results obtained in studying the photoconductive property of Se nanowire [26], which indicates that there was almost no current under irradiation if the Se nanowire was not applied bias voltage.

For rGQDs/SeNWs composite, as shown on curve (c), very strong photocurrent (about 550μA/cm2) was produced under irradiation, which resulted from the synergistic action of both rGQDs and Se NWs as follows. From electronic structure, the rGQDs are electron-deficient particles and the Se NWs are electron-rich particles. Therefore, the photo-exited electrons in Se NWs moved into the rGQDs leading to produce an electric field at the graphene quantum dot/Se nanowire interface where the Schottky barrier was formed as a result. The Schottky barrier could hinder the recombination of photo-exited electrons with holes. In addition, the Schottky barrier height is expected to decline since the Fermi level in the Se nanowire is raised under irradiation [26], which is in favor of electron transfer from Se nanowire to graphene quantum dot from which the photo-exited electrons were further transferred to ITO.

Meanwhile, by way of the Se nanowire that is a kind of p-type semiconductor whose conduction occurs due to hole transport of the valence band [50], the produced holes were transferred to the electrode-electrolyte interface where the holes were neutralized with the electrons provided by electroactive species in solution. In addition, as shown in Fig. 2A, the increase of photo-activation and red-shift of absorption edge for rGQDs/SeNWs composite promoted the formation of photocurrent. Finally, it is worth pointing out that the irradiation not only excited the electrons in Se nanowire, but also enhanced the conduction of Se nanowire with high photoconductivity (10−5 Ω/cm) [51]. The photoelectric conversion mechanism of the rGQDs/SeNWs composite is illustrated in Fig. 3B.

To get insight into the effect factors on the photoelectric conversion of the rGQDs/SeNWs composite, a series of experiments about electrodeposition potential and time, concentration of CTAB and H2SeO3 in electrolyte solution, and redox couple in electrolyte solution, were performed. A part of test results are shown in Figs. 4 and 5
.

From the test results as above, it is concluded that the electrodeposition conditions affected the morphology of the products and then the photoelectric conversion of the products. Presumably, excellent photoelectric conversion of the rGQDs/SeNWs composite obtained in typical condition resulted from its uniform compositing 1D structure. As shown in Fig. 5D, when KI solution was used as electrolyte solution for photoelectric conversion, the photocurrents produced by the products were decreased comparing with that obtained in typical electrolyte (Na2S) condition. According to the results reported [52], the I−/I3− redox couple is corrosive to quantum dots, which resulted in the decrease of the photocurrent.

Finally, for the sake of evaluating the photocurrent stability of the products, the photocurrents under continuous irradiation were measured, and the results are shown in Fig. 6 A. Clearly, strong photocurrent produced by rGQDs/SeNWs composite falls slowly going with time extension and its intensity can keep above 0.5mA/cm2 until 1000s. Fig. 6B shows the change of the photocurrents generated by the products at different bias potentials. From the current–voltage (I-V) curves, it is obtained that the open-circuit voltages (namely, the voltage at zero current) are −0. 1V, −0.79V and −1.01V for Se NWs, rGQDs and rGQDs/SeNWs composite, respectively. Obviously, rGQDs/SeNWs composite not only produced large current (over 500μA/cm2) but also provided high voltage (over 1V) due to the synergistic action of electroconductive rGQDs and photosensitive Se NWs. In literatures, it has been reported that the polymer-based bulk-heterojunction photovoltaic material (PolyPLY/PCBM) exhibited a short circuit current density of 390μA/cm2 and an open circuit voltageof 0.86V [53]. The porphyrin-[60]fullerene polymer linked by metal axial coordination yielded an open-circuit voltage (Voc) of 0.94V, and a steady and rapid photocurrent response (about 10.27nA) [54], and the graphene quantum dots surface-passivated by polyethylene glycol (GQDs-PEG) produced dozens of nA/cm2 [55]. Clearly, our rGQDs/SeNWs composite has better photoelectric conversion performance and holds great promise for the fabrication of high-efficiency light energy conversion systems.

In summary, a novel rGQDs/SeNWs composite was synthesized by one-step electrodeposition in electrolyte solution containing H2SeO3, CTAB and GQDs. The precursor GQDs were prepared by electrooxidation of graphite rod. The morphology and crystallization of the products were characterized, and the results show that the composite was made up of trigonal phase Se NWs wrapped by rGQDs. Dependence on the synergistic effect of the photosensitivity of Se NWs and the electroconductivity of rGQDs, the rGQDs/SeNWs composite showed excellent photoelectric conversion performance. The findings are significant to the development of highly efficiency photoelectronic devices. which makes it a good candidate for the electrode material of solar cell.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: Support for this work from the National Natural Science Foundation of China (Nos. 21275006, 21471001, 21071002, 20905001, 21175001, 51432001) is gratefully acknowledged.
